docs.title: RForge Plugin
docs.text:
R package ecosystem orchestrator for Claude Code — 16 commands, R-aware hooks, validation skills.

Self-contained R package analysis for Claude Code. As of v1.3.0 the plugin is fully self-sufficient — pure-Python lib/ modules handle discovery, dependencies, status, and init. No MCP server required.

docs.title: What's new in v1.3.0
docs.text:
• 🎯 MCP absorption complete — rforge-mcp has been absorbed into the plugin. All 7 implemented tools now ship as pure-Python lib/ modules. See migration guide.
  • 🐍 lib/status.py — ecosystem health snapshot (DESCRIPTION + .STATUS parsing). python3 -m lib.status.
  • 🌱 lib/init.py — ~/.rforge/context.json initializer. New /rforge:init command.
  • 📦 No runtime dependencies beyond Python 3.10+.

docs.title: What's new in v1.2.0
docs.text:
• 🛒 Marketplace install — /plugin marketplace add Data-Wise/rforge
  • 🪝 R-aware PreToolUse hook — 4 rules (block man/*.Rd edits, warn on R/*.R and DESCRIPTION SemVer drift, warn on outside-worktree writes). See Hooks & Skills.
  • 🔍 description-sync validation skill — pure-shell DESCRIPTION ↔ NEWS.md drift check. No R required.
  • 📐 Plugin Surface diagram in Architecture (Mermaid).
  • 🔓 MCP decoupled — npm install works without rforge-mcp (was failing with 404 for fresh users). Absorbed entirely in v1.3.0.
  • ⚙️ User options — see Configuration for cran_mirror, vignette_engine, r_version_pin, claude_md_budget.

Full release notes: CHANGELOG.md.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
# Quick analysis (< 30 seconds)
/rforge:analyze "Update RMediation bootstrap algorithm"

# Ultra-fast (< 10 seconds)
/rforge:quick

# Comprehensive (2-5 minutes)
/rforge:thorough "Prepare for CRAN release"

docs.title: Features
docs.text: ✨ Auto-delegation - Recognizes task patterns, selects appropriate tools ⚡ Parallel execution - Calls multiple MCP tools simultaneously 📊 Live progress - Real-time updates as tools complete 🎯 Smart synthesis - Combines results into actionable summary 🧠 ADHD-friendly - Fast feedback, clear structure, visual progress

docs.text:
User Request
    ↓
Pattern Recognition (CODE_CHANGE, BUG_FIX, etc.)
    ↓
Tool Selection (impact, tests, docs, health)
    ↓
Parallel MCP Calls (4 tools × 8 sec = 8 sec total, not 32 sec!)
    ↓
Results Synthesis (impact + quality + maintenance + next steps)

Actionable Summary

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
1. Python 3.10+ — lib/ modules run via python3 -m lib.<tool>
  2. R Environment
  3. R >= 4.0.0
  4. devtools (optional, for /rforge:thorough)
  5. testthat (optional)
  6. covr (optional, for coverage)
  7. Claude Code - This is a Claude Code plugin

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
/plugin marketplace add Data-Wise/rforge
/plugin install rforge

Alternative options (Homebrew, npm, manual symlink) are documented in the main README.

Migrating from v1.2.x? If ~/.claude/settings.json has an mcpServers.rforge entry, it's no longer needed in v1.3.0 — remove it manually. See migration guide.

  1. Restart Claude Code

  2. Test it 

    cd /path/to/r-package
/rforge:analyze "Test installation"

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
"python3: command not found" 

# Verify Python 3.10+ is on PATH
python3 --version

# macOS: install via Homebrew
brew install python@3.12

"Package not detected" 

# Run from package directory
cd /path/to/package

# Or specify path explicitly
/rforge:analyze --package /path/to/package

"Analysis too slow" - Use /rforge:quick for fast status - Use /rforge:analyze for balanced speed/depth - Only use /rforge:thorough when needed

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Plugin settings in plugin.json:

{
  "settings": {
    "default_mode": "quick",           // quick, analyze, or thorough
    "parallel_execution": true,         // Run tools in parallel
    "show_progress": true,              // Show progress bars
    "auto_synthesize": true             // Auto-generate summary
  }
}

docs.title: Pattern Recognition
docs.text:
The orchestrator automatically detects what you're doing:

"update algorithm" → CODE_CHANGE pattern → Calls: impact, tests, docs

docs.title: Tips for Best Results
docs.text:
1. Describe tasks clearly
  2. Good: /rforge:analyze "Updated bootstrap algorithm in mediate()"

  3. Less good: /rforge:analyze "changed code"

  4. Use right command for context

  5. Pre-commit → /rforge:quick
  6. After changes → /rforge:analyze

  7. Before release → /rforge:thorough

  8. Must be in R package directory

  9. Needs DESCRIPTION file

  10. Run from package root

  11. Parallel execution is automatic

  12. No configuration needed
  13. Much faster than sequential tool calls

docs.text:
Commands not showing? - Restart Claude Code - Check: ls ~/.claude/plugins/rforge

"python3: command not found"? - Verify Python 3.10+ is installed: python3 --version - macOS: brew install python@3.12 - Linux: install via your distro's package manager

Slow performance? - Run /rforge:quick for the fastest tool - For thorough analysis, expect 2-5 minutes (/rforge:thorough) - Check that you're in the package root (not a subdirectory)

docs.title: Example Session
docs.text:
# In your R package directory
cd ~/projects/r-packages/mypackage

# Quick check before starting work
/rforge:quick
# → ✅ Package healthy, 95% test coverage

# Make changes
vim R/bootstrap.R

# Analyze impact
/rforge:analyze "Updated bootstrap algorithm for better performance"
# → Impact: Modified 1 function, affects 3 others
# → Tests: All pass, coverage maintained
# → Docs: Update help file for bootstrap_se()

# Fix documentation
vim man/bootstrap_se.Rd

# Quick verify
/rforge:quick
# → ✅ All clear!

# Commit
git commit -m "feat: improve bootstrap algorithm performance"

You're ready! Try /rforge:quick in your R package directory to get started.

docs.title: Pattern Recognition
docs.text:
The orchestrator automatically detects task types:

Pattern Triggers When... Tools Called CODE_CHANGE "update", "modify", "change code" impact, tests, docs BUG_FIX "fix", "bug", "error" impact, tests RELEASE_PREP "release", "CRAN", "publish" health, tests, docs, rdoc HEALTH_CHECK "check", "status", "quick" health FEATURE_ADD "add feature", "new function" impact, tests, docs

docs.text:
Tools the orchestrator can delegate to:

Tool Purpose Speed impact Analyze code change impact ~8s tests Run and analyze tests ~10s docs Check documentation completeness ~8s health Package health metrics ~8s rdoc R documentation validation ~8s

docs.title: Hooks & Skills (v1.2.0+)
docs.text:
RForge ships autonomous tooling that activates without an explicit slash command.

pretooluse.py hook — runs on every Write/Edit:

Rule Severity Edit man/*.Rd (roxygen-generated) BLOCK Edit R/*.R warn (NAMESPACE/DESCRIPTION reminder) Bad SemVer in DESCRIPTION Version: warn Write outside current worktree warn

skills/validation/description-sync.md — verifies DESCRIPTION Version: matches the top entry of NEWS.md / CHANGELOG.md. Pure shell, no R required. Runs in release mode pre-CRAN.

See Hooks & Skills Reference for full details.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
Claude Code marketplace (Recommended, v1.2.0+):

From inside Claude Code:

/plugin marketplace add Data-Wise/rforge
/plugin install rforge

Update later with /plugin update rforge. Works on macOS, Linux, Windows.

Homebrew (macOS):

brew install data-wise/tap/rforge

Manual:

git clone https://github.com/Data-Wise/rforge.git
ln -s "$(pwd)/rforge" ~/.claude/plugins/rforge

See the main README for the full matrix of install options.

docs.title: RForge Architecture Guide
docs.text: Deep dive into how RForge works internally: auto-delegation orchestration, pattern recognition, parallel execution, and result synthesis.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
RForge is an orchestrator plugin that intelligently delegates to RForge MCP server tools. It doesn't execute R code directly - instead, it acts as an intelligent coordinator that recognizes patterns, selects appropriate tools, executes them in parallel, and synthesizes results.

Key Innovation: Auto-delegation with parallel execution that completes 4 tool calls in the time it takes to execute 1.

docs.title: Plugin Surface
docs.text:
Added in v1.2.0. Mirrors craft's .claude-plugin/ layout.

The plugin ships four kinds of artifact, each loaded by Claude Code at a different lifecycle stage:

graph LR
    subgraph User["You / Claude Code session"]
        UR[User request]
    end

    subgraph Plugin[".claude-plugin/"]
        MP[marketplace.json<br/><i>install-time</i>]
        CFG[config.json<br/><i>plugin-load</i>]
        CMD[commands/*.md<br/><i>15 slash commands</i>]
        AGT[agents/orchestrator.md<br/><i>auto-delegation</i>]
        HK[hooks/pretooluse.py<br/><i>every Write/Edit</i>]
        SK[skills/validation/*.md<br/><i>auto-discovered</i>]
    end

    subgraph MCP["RForge MCP server"]
        T1[health]
        T2[tests]
        T3[docs]
        T4[impact]
    end

    subgraph R["R environment"]
        DESCR[DESCRIPTION]
        NS[NAMESPACE]
        RD[man/*.Rd]
        SRC[R/*.R]
    end

    UR -->|/rforge:analyze| CMD
    UR -->|edits files| HK
    HK -->|reads| DESCR
    HK -->|protects| RD
    CMD -->|delegates| AGT
    AGT -->|invokes| T1
    AGT -->|invokes| T2
    AGT -->|invokes| T3
    AGT -->|invokes| T4
    T1 --> R
    T2 --> R
    T3 --> R
    T4 --> R
    SK -.->|optional| DESCR
    CFG -.->|reads| CMD
    CFG -.->|reads| AGT
Layer When it fires Lives in Authority marketplace.json At install (/plugin marketplace add) .claude-plugin/ Claude Code marketplace config.json When plugin loads .claude-plugin/ rforge runtime commands/*.md When user types /rforge:<cmd> commands/ Claude Code agents/orchestrator.md When pattern recognition triggers agents/ Claude Code hooks/pretooluse.py Every Write/Edit tool call .claude-plugin/hooks/ Claude Code hook system skills/validation/*.md When Claude needs to verify state .claude-plugin/skills/ Claude Code skill discovery

The orchestrator-and-MCP machinery (covered in the rest of this document) is the runtime brain. The four pieces above are the static surface the plugin advertises to Claude Code.

For a user-facing tour of the new hook + skill, see Hooks & Skills Reference.

docs.title: Path B: lib/ modules
docs.text: Added in v1.3.0. Three former MCP tools (rforge_detect, rforge_deps, rforge_impact) ported to self-contained Python in lib/. Path A (v1.2.0) dropped the peer dependency on rforge-mcp; Path B absorbs the logic. The MCP server is still supported — lib/ runs in parallel.

docs.title: Two execution paths
docs.text:
Commands like /rforge:detect and /rforge:deps now have a choice: they can shell out to the in-plugin Python modules (no external service required) or, if the user has rforge-mcp installed, delegate to the MCP server. The output shape is equivalent for the three ported tools.

graph TB
    subgraph User["You / Claude Code session"]
        UR[User: /rforge:detect or /rforge:deps]
    end

    subgraph Cmd[commands/*.md]
        DT[detect.md]
        DP[deps.md]
        IM[impact.md]
    end

    subgraph LibPath["Path B (v1.3+, default)"]
        L1[lib/discovery.py]
        L2[lib/deps.py]
    end

    subgraph MCPPath["MCP path (still supported)"]
        T1[rforge_detect]
        T2[rforge_deps]
        T3[rforge_impact]
    end

    subgraph FS["Filesystem"]
        DESCR[R DESCRIPTION files]
        CFG[.rforge.yaml]
    end

    UR --> DT
    UR --> DP
    UR --> IM
    DT -->|bash python3| L1
    DP -->|bash python3| L2
    IM -->|bash python3| L2
    DT -.->|optional MCP| T1
    DP -.->|optional MCP| T2
    IM -.->|optional MCP| T3
    L1 --> DESCR
    L1 --> CFG
    L2 --> DESCR
    T1 --> DESCR
    T2 --> DESCR
    T3 --> DESCR

Solid arrows: the in-plugin path (v1.3+ default). Dashed arrows: the legacy MCP path, still functional for users who installed rforge-mcp.

docs.title: Why two paths?
docs.text:
• Non-breaking migration. Existing users keep working without forced reinstall; new users get pure-Python analysis with zero peer deps.
  • Capability ceiling. Commands that need R subprocess (e.g., /rforge:status with optimize/release modes) still rely on the MCP server until Phase B.2 ports status to lib/status.py. See the Path B SPEC for the full plan.
  • Wire compatibility. lib/discovery.py preserves the MCP server's mode field (minimal/standard/full) for direct side-by-side validation against rforge_detect output. The user-facing kind field (single/ecosystem/hybrid) is layered on top.

docs.title: What's still MCP-only
docs.text:
Tool Phase Status rforge_status B.2 Pending — mode-aware execution (default/debug/optimize/release) with R subprocess rforge_init B.3 Pending — .rforge/context.json state file

See docs/lib-modules.md for the user-facing reference, and the auto-extracted reference/discovery.md / reference/deps.md for full API listings.

docs.title: Pattern Recognition
docs.text: RForge recognizes 6 primary task patterns:

docs.title: 1. CODE_CHANGE
docs.text:
Triggers: "updated", "modified", "changed code", "implemented"

Selected Tools: - rforge-mcp.impact - Assess change impact - rforge-mcp.tests - Verify tests still pass - rforge-mcp.docs - Check if docs need updating

Example: 

User: "I updated the bootstrap function in RMediation"
→ Pattern: CODE_CHANGE
→ Tools: [impact, tests, docs]
→ Output: "3 packages affected, 42 tests passing, docs up-to-date"

docs.title: 2. BUG_FIX
docs.text:
Triggers: "bug", "error", "fix", "issue"

Selected Tools: - rforge-mcp.tests - Verify fix works - rforge-mcp.regression - Check for regressions - rforge-mcp.health - Overall package health

Example: 

User: "Fixed edge case in mediation calculation"
→ Pattern: BUG_FIX
→ Tools: [tests, regression, health]
→ Output: "Fix verified, no regressions, health score: 85/100"

docs.title: 3. CRAN_RELEASE
docs.text:
Triggers: "CRAN", "release", "submit", "publish"

Selected Tools: - rforge-mcp.check - R CMD check - rforge-mcp.docs - Documentation completeness - rforge-mcp.deps - Dependency validation - rforge-mcp.health - Overall readiness

Example: 

User: "Prepare for CRAN submission"
→ Pattern: CRAN_RELEASE
→ Tools: [check, docs, deps, health]
→ Output: "3 warnings to address, docs 95% complete, ready in 1-2 hours"

docs.title: 4. DOCUMENTATION
docs.text:
Triggers: "document", "docs", "README", "vignette"

Selected Tools: - rforge-mcp.docs - Documentation status - rforge-mcp.examples - Runnable examples check - rforge-mcp.vignettes - Vignette validation

Example: 

User: "Update documentation for new features"
→ Pattern: DOCUMENTATION
→ Tools: [docs, examples, vignettes]
→ Output: "2 functions undocumented, 3 examples need updating"

docs.title: 5. DEPENDENCY_UPDATE
docs.text:
Triggers: "dependency", "upgrade", "version bump", "import"

Selected Tools: - rforge-mcp.deps - Dependency analysis - rforge-mcp.impact - Cross-package impact - rforge-mcp.cascade - Update planning

Example: 

User: "Updated ggplot2 dependency to v3.5.0"
→ Pattern: DEPENDENCY_UPDATE
→ Tools: [deps, impact, cascade]
→ Output: "5 packages affected, update order: base → extension1 → extension2"

docs.title: 6. GENERAL_STATUS
docs.text:
Triggers: "status", "health", "check", "overview"

Selected Tools: - rforge-mcp.health - Overall health - rforge-mcp.git - Git status - rforge-mcp.tests - Test summary

Example: 

User: "What's the current status?"
→ Pattern: GENERAL_STATUS
→ Tools: [health, git, tests]
→ Output: "Health: 85/100, main branch clean, 42/45 tests passing"

docs.title: Why Parallel Execution Matters
docs.text:
Sequential Execution (Old Way): 

Tool 1: 8 seconds
Tool 2: 8 seconds
Tool 3: 8 seconds
Tool 4: 8 seconds
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Total:  32 seconds ❌

Parallel Execution (RForge Way): 

Tool 1: ░░░░░░░░ (8s)
Tool 2: ░░░░░░░░ (8s)  ← All run simultaneously
Tool 3: ░░░░░░░░ (8s)
Tool 4: ░░░░░░░░ (8s)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Total:  ~8 seconds ✅

Performance gain: 4× faster (or more with additional tools)

docs.title: Implementation
docs.text:
RForge uses Claude Code's Task tool to launch multiple background agents:

// Pseudo-code representation
const tools = selectTools(pattern);  // ["impact", "tests", "docs"]

// Launch all tools in parallel
const results = await Promise.all(
  tools.map(tool => executeToolInBackground(tool))
);

// Synthesize when all complete
const summary = synthesizeResults(results);

docs.title: Real-World Performance
docs.text:
From Phase 1 testing on mediationverse ecosystem (5 R packages):

Metric Value Average execution 4ms Maximum execution 9ms Tools called 4 simultaneously Speedup 1,250× under target

Note: These are orchestration times. Actual MCP tool execution happens asynchronously in background.

docs.title: Mode System Integration
docs.text: RForge's mode system controls which tools are called and how detailed the analysis is:

docs.title: Default Mode (<10s)
docs.text:
Tools: Lightweight status checks only 

[health (lite), git, quick-test]
Total: 3 tools, ~8 seconds

docs.title: Debug Mode (<120s)
docs.text:
Tools: Detailed diagnostics 

[health (full), git, tests (verbose), logs, traces]
Total: 5-6 tools, ~90 seconds

docs.title: Optimize Mode (<180s)
docs.text:
Tools: Performance profiling 

[health, tests, benchmark, profiler, bottlenecks]
Total: 5-7 tools, ~150 seconds

docs.title: Release Mode (<300s)
docs.text:
Tools: Comprehensive audit 

[check, tests (full), coverage, docs, deps, health, examples, vignettes]
Total: 8+ tools, ~240 seconds

Mode selection logic: 

if (userSpecifiedMode) {
  use userSpecifiedMode
} else if (pattern === CRAN_RELEASE) {
  use 'release' mode
} else if (pattern === BUG_FIX && contextHasFailures) {
  use 'debug' mode
} else {
  use 'default' mode
}

docs.title: Result Synthesis
docs.text: After parallel execution, RForge synthesizes results into a unified, actionable summary.

docs.title: Synthesis Algorithm
docs.text:
1. Collect Results
Tool 1 (impact): "3 packages affected"
Tool 2 (tests):  "42/45 passing (3 failures)"
Tool 3 (docs):   "95% documented"
Tool 4 (health): "Score: 85/100"
  1. Extract Key Findings
  2. Critical issues (red flags)
  3. Warnings (yellow flags)

  4. Success indicators (green flags)

  5. Generate Summary

  6. Overall health assessment
  7. Priority action items
  8. Quick wins available

  9. Long-term recommendations

  10. Format for Output

  11. Terminal: Rich colors, emojis, tables
  12. JSON: Structured with metadata
  13. Markdown: Documentation-ready

docs.title: Example Synthesis
docs.text:
Input (4 tool results): 

{
  "impact": {"affected_packages": 3, "breaking_changes": 0},
  "tests": {"passing": 42, "failing": 3, "coverage": 78},
  "docs": {"documented": 38, "total": 40, "percentage": 95},
  "health": {"score": 85, "warnings": 2, "errors": 0}
}

Output (Terminal): 

╭──────────────────────────────────────────╮
│ 📊 RForge Analysis Summary               │
├──────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                          │
│ Health Score: 85/100 ✅                  │
│                                          │
│ 🎯 Impact:                               │
│    • 3 packages affected                 │
│    • No breaking changes                 │
│                                          │
│ ✅ Tests: 42/45 passing                  │
│ ⚠️  Coverage: 78% (target: 80%)          │
│                                          │
│ 📚 Docs: 95% complete                    │
│    • 2 functions need documentation      │
│                                          │
│ 🔧 Next Steps:                           │
│    1. Fix 3 failing tests (priority)     │
│    2. Add coverage for edge cases        │
│    3. Document remaining 2 functions     │
│                                          │
╰──────────────────────────────────────────╯

docs.title: MCP Tool Integration
docs.text:
RForge delegates to these RForge MCP server tools:

Tool Purpose Typical Time rforge-mcp.health Package health metrics 2-3s rforge-mcp.tests Test execution status 5-10s rforge-mcp.coverage Test coverage analysis 8-15s rforge-mcp.docs Documentation completeness 3-5s rforge-mcp.deps Dependency analysis 2-4s rforge-mcp.impact Change impact assessment 4-8s rforge-mcp.check R CMD check execution 30-180s rforge-mcp.git Git status 1s

Communication: 

RForge Plugin → Task Tool → RForge MCP Server → R Environment

docs.title: Project Structure Detection
docs.text: RForge auto-detects three project types:

docs.title: Detection Algorithm
docs.text:
def detect_project_type(directory):
    has_description = find_files("DESCRIPTION")

    if len(has_description) == 0:
        return "NOT_R_PROJECT"
    elif len(has_description) == 1:
        return "SINGLE_PACKAGE"
    elif all_in_subdirs(has_description):
        return "ECOSYSTEM"
    else:
        return "HYBRID"

docs.title: Ecosystem Detection Benefits
docs.text:
Once RForge knows project type, it can: - Single Package: Focus on that package only - Ecosystem: Analyze dependencies and cross-package impact - Hybrid: Intelligently identify R packages among other content

Example: 

mediationverse/
├── RMediation/DESCRIPTION      ← Package 1
├── bmem/DESCRIPTION            ← Package 2
├── regmedint/DESCRIPTION       ← Package 3
└── pomeMediation/DESCRIPTION   ← Package 4

→ Detected: ECOSYSTEM (4 packages)
→ Enables: cascade, impact, release planning

docs.title: Error Handling & Resilience
docs.text: RForge implements robust error handling:

docs.title: Tool Failure Handling
docs.text:
If one tool fails: 

Tool 1: ✅ Success
Tool 2: ❌ Timeout
Tool 3: ✅ Success
Tool 4: ✅ Success

→ RForge continues with 3 results
→ Notes "Tool 2 unavailable" in output
→ Still provides actionable summary

docs.title: Graceful Degradation
docs.text:
Priority levels: 1. Critical: health, git status (must succeed) 2. Important: tests, docs (try hard) 3. Optional: coverage, profiling (nice to have)

Strategy: 

if (critical_tool_fails) {
  retry_with_backoff()
  if (still_fails) {
    return error_to_user
  }
} else if (important_tool_fails) {
  continue_without_it
  note_in_output("Some data unavailable")
} else {
  // optional tool - just skip
}

docs.title: 1. Caching
docs.text:
RForge caches frequently accessed data:

// Cache package metadata (5 min TTL)
cache.set(`package:${name}:metadata`, metadata, 300);

// Cache test results (until code changes)
cache.set(`package:${name}:tests`, results, UNTIL_CHANGE);

// Cache dependency graph (1 hour TTL)
cache.set(`ecosystem:deps`, graph, 3600);

docs.title: 2. Smart Tool Selection
docs.text:
Only calls necessary tools:

// Skip coverage if no tests changed
if (!testsChanged) {
  skip('coverage');
}

// Skip docs check if no exported functions changed
if (!exportsChanged) {
  skip('docs');
}

docs.title: 3. Incremental Analysis
docs.text:
For ecosystems, analyzes only changed packages:

const changedPackages = git.getChangedPackages();
const affectedPackages = deps.findAffected(changedPackages);

// Only analyze changed + affected (not entire ecosystem)
analyze(changedPackages.concat(affectedPackages));

docs.title: Output Format System
docs.text: RForge supports 3 output formats, implemented as formatters:

docs.title: Terminal Formatter
docs.text:
class TerminalFormatter {
  format(data) {
    return {
      colors: chalk,      // Rich colors
      emojis: true,       // Visual indicators
      tables: true,       // Formatted tables
      boxes: true         // Unicode boxes
    };
  }
}

docs.title: JSON Formatter
docs.text:
class JSONFormatter {
  format(data) {
    return {
      metadata: {
        timestamp: ISO8601,
        version: "1.0.0",
        mode: "default"
      },
      data: structuredData
    };
  }
}

docs.title: Markdown Formatter
docs.text:
class MarkdownFormatter {
  format(data) {
    return {
      headings: H1/H2/H3,
      codeBlocks: "```",
      tables: markdown,
      links: true
    };
  }
}

docs.title: Planned Improvements
docs.text:
1. Streaming Results
  2. Show results as tools complete (instead of waiting for all)

  3. Better user feedback during long operations

  4. Predictive Tool Selection

  5. Learn from past analyses to predict needed tools

  6. Reduce unnecessary tool calls

  7. Distributed Execution

  8. Execute tools across multiple machines

docs.title: RForge Commands Reference
docs.text: Complete reference for all 15 RForge commands. Commands are organized by category with usage examples and parameter details.

docs.title: Command Categories
docs.text:
• Status & Analysis (4 commands)
  • Ecosystem Management (5 commands)
  • Documentation & Tasks (4 commands)
  • Health Checks (2 commands)

docs.title: /rforge:status
docs.text:
Quick ecosystem-wide status dashboard with mode-specific detail levels.

Usage: 

/rforge:status [package] [--mode MODE] [--format FORMAT]

Parameters: - package (optional) - Package name (defaults to current or ecosystem) - --mode (optional) - Status detail level: default, debug, optimize, release - --format (optional) - Output format: terminal, json, markdown

Examples: 

# Quick health check (default mode)
/rforge:status

# Debug mode for detailed diagnostics
/rforge:status --mode debug

# JSON output for automation
/rforge:status --format json

# Specific package status
/rforge:status mypackage --mode release

Output includes: - Health score (0-100) - Package version and path - Git status - Dependencies status - Test results - Coverage metrics

Time budget: <10s (default), up to 300s (release mode)

docs.title: /rforge:analyze
docs.text:
Deep analysis with mode system support and intelligent recommendations.

Usage: 

/rforge:analyze [description] [--mode MODE] [--format FORMAT]

Parameters: - description (optional) - What to analyze or focus on - --mode (optional) - Analysis depth: default, debug, optimize, release - --format (optional) - Output format: terminal, json, markdown

Examples: 

# Balanced analysis
/rforge:analyze "Check package health"

# Debug mode for investigating issues
/rforge:analyze --mode debug "Why are tests failing?"

# Release mode for CRAN preparation
/rforge:analyze --mode release "Prepare for CRAN submission"

# Performance analysis
/rforge:analyze --mode optimize "Identify bottlenecks"

Performs: - Pattern recognition (CODE_CHANGE, BUG_FIX, CRAN_RELEASE, etc.) - Tool selection based on context - Parallel MCP execution - Results synthesis with actionable recommendations

Time budget: <30s (default), up to 300s (release mode)

docs.title: /rforge:quick
docs.text:
Ultra-fast status check using only quick tools (<10 seconds).

Usage: 

/rforge:quick [description]

Parameters: - description (optional) - Brief context for the check

Examples: 

# Morning stand-up check
/rforge:quick

# Pre-commit validation
/rforge:quick "Before committing changes"

Provides: - Basic package info - Git status - Critical issues only - No deep analysis

Time budget: <10s (guaranteed)

docs.title: /rforge:thorough
docs.text:
Comprehensive analysis with R CMD check integration (2-5 minutes).

Usage: 

/rforge:thorough [description]

Parameters: - description (optional) - Analysis context

Examples: 

# Full CRAN preparation
/rforge:thorough "Prepare for CRAN submission"

# Deep quality audit
/rforge:thorough "Pre-release audit"

Includes: - R CMD check execution - Full dependency tree analysis - Complete test suite with coverage - Documentation completeness check - All quality metrics

Time budget: 2-5 minutes

docs.title: /rforge:detect
docs.text:
Auto-detect project structure (single package, ecosystem, or hybrid).

Usage: 

/rforge:detect

No parameters

Examples: 

# Detect current directory structure
/rforge:detect

Output: - Project type: single, ecosystem, or hybrid - Package count and names - Directory structure summary - Dependency relationships

docs.title: /rforge:cascade
docs.text:
Plan coordinated updates across dependent packages.

Usage: 

/rforge:cascade [description]

Parameters: - description (optional) - What's changing and why

Examples: 

# Plan update cascade
/rforge:cascade "Update base package dependency from v1 to v2"

# Version bump cascade
/rforge:cascade "Major version bump in core package"

Analyzes: - Dependency graph - Update order (respecting dependencies) - Breaking changes impact - Test coverage for affected packages

docs.title: /rforge:impact
docs.text:
Analyze change impact across ecosystem packages.

Usage: 

/rforge:impact [description]

Parameters: - description (optional) - What changed

Examples: 

# API change impact
/rforge:impact "Changed function signature in base package"

# Dependency update impact
/rforge:impact "Updated ggplot2 to v3.5.0"

Reports: - Affected packages - Breaking vs non-breaking changes - Required updates - Test implications

docs.title: /rforge:release
docs.text:
Plan CRAN submission sequence based on dependencies.

Usage: 

/rforge:release

No parameters

Examples: 

# Generate release plan
/rforge:release

Provides: - Submission order (base packages first) - Pre-submission checklist per package - Version compatibility check - Timeline estimate

docs.title: /rforge:deps
docs.text:
Build and visualize dependency graph across R package ecosystem.

Usage: 

/rforge:deps [--format FORMAT]

Parameters: - --format (optional) - Output format: terminal, json, mermaid

Examples: 

# Terminal visualization
/rforge:deps

# Mermaid diagram for documentation
/rforge:deps --format mermaid > deps.mmd

# JSON for programmatic use
/rforge:deps --format json

Output: - Dependency graph (directed acyclic graph) - Import vs Suggest relationships - Circular dependency detection - External dependencies

docs.title: /rforge:doc-check
docs.text:
Check for documentation drift and inconsistencies across packages.

Usage: 

/rforge:doc-check

No parameters

Examples: 

# Check documentation status
/rforge:doc-check

Validates: - Exported functions have documentation - Examples are runnable - NAMESPACE consistency with documentation - Version numbers match across files

docs.title: /rforge:complete
docs.text:
Mark tasks complete with automatic documentation cascade.

Usage: 

/rforge:complete [task_description]

Parameters: - task_description (optional) - What was completed

Examples: 

# Mark task complete
/rforge:complete "Implemented bootstrap CI method"

# Trigger doc cascade
/rforge:complete "Fixed critical bug in mediation function"

Actions: - Updates task tracking - Triggers documentation updates - Updates CHANGELOG if configured - Git commit prompt (optional)

docs.title: /rforge:capture
docs.text:
Quick capture ideas and tasks for later (with automatic doc cascade detection).

Usage: 

/rforge:capture [idea]

Parameters: - idea (required) - What to capture

Examples: 

# Capture feature idea
/rforge:capture "Add support for weighted mediation"

# Capture bug note
/rforge:capture "Edge case: NA handling in bootstrap"

Stores: - Idea with timestamp - Context (current package, branch) - Suggested next actions

docs.title: /rforge:next
docs.text:
Get ecosystem-aware next task recommendation.

Usage: 

/rforge:next

No parameters

Examples: 

# Get next task suggestion
/rforge:next

Considers: - Unfinished tasks - Blocking issues - Dependency order - Quick wins - Priority indicators

Output: - ONE recommended task - WHY this task (reasoning) - Time estimate - 2-3 alternatives

docs.title: /rforge:ecosystem-health
docs.text:
Comprehensive ecosystem health metrics with visual dashboard.

Usage: 

/rforge:ecosystem-health [--format FORMAT]

Parameters: - --format (optional) - Output format: terminal, json, markdown

Examples: 

# Visual health dashboard
/rforge:ecosystem-health

# JSON metrics
/rforge:ecosystem-health --format json

Metrics: - Overall health score (0-100) - Per-package scores - Test coverage distribution - Dependency freshness - Documentation completeness

docs.title: /rforge:rpkg-check
docs.text:
R CMD check integration with detailed error reporting.

Usage: 

/rforge:rpkg-check [package]

Parameters: - package (optional) - Package to check (defaults to current)

Examples: 

# Check current package
/rforge:rpkg-check

# Check specific package
/rforge:rpkg-check mypackage

Executes: - R CMD check with standard flags - Parses output for errors/warnings/notes - Categorizes issues - Suggests fixes

Note: This can take 1-5 minutes depending on package size.

docs.title: By Time Budget
docs.text: Time Commands <10s status, quick, detect, deps (visual), next <30s analyze (default), cascade, impact, doc-check <2min analyze (debug/optimize) <5min thorough, analyze (release), rpkg-check

docs.title: By Use Case
docs.text:

Feature Development: - /rforge:analyze - Check implementation impact - /rforge:impact - Assess ecosystem effects - /rforge:complete - Mark features done

docs.title: Global Flags
docs.text:
All analysis commands support:

Mode flags: - --mode default - Quick analysis (<10s) - --mode debug - Detailed diagnostics (<120s) - --mode optimize - Performance focus (<180s) - --mode release - Comprehensive audit (<300s)

Format flags: - --format terminal - Rich colors and emojis (default) - --format json - Machine-readable with metadata - --format markdown - Documentation-ready

Example: 

/rforge:analyze --mode release --format markdown > analysis.md

docs.title: Configuring RForge for Your R Package
docs.text:
Added in v1.2.0. Stub configuration; more options will be added in later phases.

RForge ships with sensible defaults, but you can tune four options through .claude-plugin/config.json. This page walks through each one and explains when you'd want to override them.

docs.title: Where configuration lives
docs.text:
File Scope Purpose .claude-plugin/config.json Plugin-shipped defaults Authoritative defaults bundled with rforge. Don't edit in place. .claude/rforge.local.json Per-project overrides Project-specific tuning. Created by you, gitignored or committed depending on team preference.

The plugin reads the bundled defaults first, then merges any project-local overrides on top.

docs.title: 1. cran_mirror
docs.text:
Default: https://cloud.r-project.org Type: URL string

The CRAN mirror used by install.packages() and dependency resolution when rforge tooling needs to fetch packages.

docs.title: When to override
docs.text: If you… Use Want vendor-neutral, globally distributed (default) https://cloud.r-project.org Are inside a Posit/RStudio shop https://cran.rstudio.com Want pre-built binary packages on Linux (much faster) https://packagemanager.posit.co/cran/latest Have an internal mirror (corporate proxy) Your internal URL

docs.title: Example override
docs.text:
{
  "cran_mirror": "https://packagemanager.posit.co/cran/latest"
}

docs.title: 2. vignette_engine
docs.text:
Default: knitr::rmarkdown Type: Enum — one of knitr::rmarkdown, knitr::knitr, quarto

The default VignetteBuilder written into DESCRIPTION when rforge scaffolds a new vignette.

docs.title: When to override
docs.text: If you… Use Build standard .Rmd vignettes (default) knitr::rmarkdown Use Quarto (.qmd) vignettes quarto Use raw .Rnw Sweave vignettes knitr::knitr

docs.title: Example override
docs.text:
{
  "vignette_engine": "quarto"
}

When you scaffold with vignette_engine: "quarto", rforge writes:

VignetteBuilder: quarto

…to DESCRIPTION and creates a vignettes/*.qmd skeleton instead of .Rmd.

docs.title: 3. r_version_pin
docs.text:
Default: >= 4.1.0 Type: SemVer comparison string

The default R version requirement written into DESCRIPTION's Depends: field when rforge scaffolds a new package.

docs.title: When to override
docs.text:
If you… Use Target broadly-installed R (default) >= 4.1.0 Need newer features (e.g. \|> native pipe) >= 4.2.0 Maintain compatibility with stale enterprise R >= 3.6.0 Pin to a specific version == 4.3.2

CRAN tooling generally prefers loose lower bounds (>=) over strict equality (==) — use == only when you have a real reason.

docs.title: Example override
docs.text:
{
  "r_version_pin": ">= 4.2.0"
}

docs.title: 4. claude_md_budget
docs.text:
Default: 600 Type: Integer (line count)

Maximum number of lines for CLAUDE.md files in your R package. /rforge:doc-check warns when this threshold is exceeded.

CLAUDE.md files at this scale tend to drift into stale documentation — 600 lines is a reasonable upper bound before they should be split into sub-files or trimmed.

docs.title: When to override
docs.text: If you… Use Want a tighter doc-budget (recommended for new projects) 400 Use the shipped default 600 Maintain a CLAUDE.md hub for a complex ecosystem 1000

docs.title: Example override
docs.text:
{
  "claude_md_budget": 400
}

docs.title: Putting it all together
docs.text:
A complete project-local override at .claude/rforge.local.json:

{
  "cran_mirror": "https://packagemanager.posit.co/cran/latest",
  "vignette_engine": "quarto",
  "r_version_pin": ">= 4.2.0",
  "claude_md_budget": 400
}

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Hooks & Skills Reference — what runs automatically on every edit.
  • .claude-plugin/config.json — the plugin-shipped defaults (read-only).
  • /rforge:status — shows the active configuration for your project.

docs.title: Hooks & Skills Reference
docs.text:
Added in v1.2.0. Mirrors craft's .claude-plugin/ layout.

RForge ships two pieces of autonomous tooling that activate without an explicit /rforge: command:

  • Hooks — fire on every Claude Code tool call (e.g. Write, Edit).
  • Skills — autonomous validators discoverable by Claude when it needs to verify package state.

This page is the user-facing reference for what's currently shipped.

docs.title: pretooluse.py
docs.text:
A PreToolUse hook that runs on every Write and Edit call. It applies four R-package-aware rules. Only one of them blocks; the rest print a warning to stderr and let the operation proceed (matches craft's "warn loudly, block rarely" philosophy).

# Rule Severity Trigger 1 Block edits to roxygen-generated .Rd files BLOCK (exit 2) file_path matches */man/*.Rd 2 Remind on R/*.R edits warn file_path matches */R/*.R 3 SemVer check on DESCRIPTION Version warn file_path ends in DESCRIPTION and Version: line is present 4 Outside-worktree write warn CWD is a git worktree, but file_path resolves outside it

docs.title: Rule 1 — Block edits to man/*.Rd
docs.text:
Hand-edits to man/*.Rd files are always wrong: those files are regenerated by devtools::document() from roxygen comments in R/. Any manual change is silently destroyed on the next regeneration. The hook blocks the call (exit 2) and prints:

🛑 Refusing to edit roxygen-generated man/*.Rd file
   File:    /path/to/man/foo.Rd
   Reason:  man/*.Rd is regenerated by devtools::document().
   Instead: edit the @param/@return/@examples roxygen comments in R/
            and run devtools::document() to regenerate.

To regenerate properly, edit the corresponding R/foo.R and run:

devtools::document()

docs.title: Rule 2 — Remind on R/*.R edits
docs.text:
Editing R source can require keeping NAMESPACE and DESCRIPTION in sync — adding @export tags, new dependencies, etc. The hook can't verify this (it can't see future edits), so it just reminds:

⚠️  Editing R/ source — remember to keep package metadata in sync
   File: /path/to/R/foo.R
   If you added/removed @export tags: run devtools::document().
   If you added a new dependency: add it to DESCRIPTION (Imports:).

docs.title: Rule 3 — SemVer check on DESCRIPTION Version
docs.text:
When writing or editing DESCRIPTION, the hook scans for a Version: line and verifies it matches MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH (with optional pre-release suffix). If it doesn't, you get:

⚠️  DESCRIPTION Version 'v1.2' is not SemVer-compatible
   Expected:  MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH (e.g. 1.2.0 or 1.2.0-rc1)
   CRAN policy: prefers strict X.Y.Z bumps for releases.

CRAN itself accepts a wider variety of version strings, but strict SemVer makes downstream packaging (Homebrew, npm, etc.) much simpler.

docs.title: Rule 4 — Outside-worktree write
docs.text:
Direct port of craft's pretooluse.py rule. When you're working in a git worktree under ~/.git-worktrees/, writes to files outside the worktree usually mean a wrong-CWD bug. The hook warns:

⚠️  Writing outside worktree
   File:     /Users/you/projects/other-repo/foo.txt
   Worktree: /Users/you/.git-worktrees/rforge/feature-x
   Consider: cd /Users/you/.git-worktrees/rforge/feature-x

docs.title: Wiring up the hook
docs.text:
The hook is not auto-wired by simply existing in the plugin. To activate, add to your ~/.claude/settings.json (or per-project .claude/settings.json):

{
  "hooks": {
    "PreToolUse": [
      {
        "matcher": "Write|Edit",
        "hooks": [
          {
            "type": "command",
            "command": "${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/.claude-plugin/hooks/pretooluse.py"
          }
        ]
      }
    ]
  }
}

docs.title: Testing the hook locally
docs.text:
Each rule can be exercised by piping JSON to stdin (Claude Code's hook contract — see .claude-plugin/hooks/README.md for details):

# Block: editing a roxygen-generated .Rd file (exit 2)
echo '{"tool_name":"Edit","tool_input":{"file_path":"man/foo.Rd","old_string":"x","new_string":"y"}}' \
  | python3 .claude-plugin/hooks/pretooluse.py
echo $?

# Warn: bad SemVer in DESCRIPTION (exit 0)
echo '{"tool_name":"Write","tool_input":{"file_path":"DESCRIPTION","content":"Version: not-semver\n"}}' \
  | python3 .claude-plugin/hooks/pretooluse.py
echo $?

docs.title: Skills
docs.text: Skills live under .claude-plugin/skills/<category>/ and are auto-discoverable by Claude based on their YAML frontmatter. Each skill is a single Markdown file.

docs.title: validation/description-sync.md
docs.text: Verifies that DESCRIPTION Version: matches the top entry of NEWS.md or CHANGELOG.md — the most common pre-release failure mode in R packages. Pure shell, no R required.

docs.title: Modes
docs.text: Mode Action on mismatch default Warn, exit 0 debug Verbose diff, exit 0 optimize Warn, exit 0 (no auto-fix — version bumps are deliberate) release Fail, exit 1

docs.title: Example output
docs.text:
✅ PASS: DESCRIPTION Version 1.2.0 found in NEWS.md

…or, if drift is detected in release mode:

❌ FAIL: DESCRIPTION Version 1.2.0 is not in NEWS.md (top 50 lines)
   Add an entry to NEWS.md before tagging the release.

docs.title: When this runs
docs.text:
• Manually, by extracting the script block and running it.
  • Auto-discovered by /rforge:check once Phase 4 (discovery engine) lands.
  • As a CRAN-prep sanity step.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Architecture Guide — where hooks and skills sit relative to the rest of the plugin.
  • Configuration Guide — user-tunable defaults via .claude-plugin/config.json.
  • REFCARD — quick reference card.
  • Source: .claude-plugin/hooks/pretooluse.py, .claude-plugin/skills/validation/description-sync.md.

docs.title: Lib Modules (lib/)
docs.text:
Pure-Python R ecosystem analysis, callable from plugin commands or directly from any shell. Ships with rforge v1.3+ (no R subprocess, no MCP server, no external dependencies beyond the stdlib).

Background. As of v1.3.0 the plugin's entire analysis surface lives in lib/ — four self-contained Python modules (discovery, deps, status, init) that replace all 7 implemented rforge-mcp tools. See Path B SPEC for the migration plan and reference docs for auto-generated API listings.

docs.title: Why this exists
docs.text:
The original analysis logic ran inside rforge-mcp (a separate Node.js server). v1.2.0 dropped the peer dependency; v1.3.0 absorbs the logic itself. Result: the plugin's commands no longer require any external service to enumerate packages, build dependency graphs, reason about change impact, summarize ecosystem health, or initialize per-user state.

flowchart LR
    cmd["/rforge:detect<br>/rforge:deps<br>/rforge:impact<br>/rforge:status<br>/rforge:init"] -->|bash| py[python3 -m lib.discovery<br>python3 -m lib.deps<br>python3 -m lib.status<br>python3 -m lib.init]
    py --> fs[(R DESCRIPTION<br>+ .STATUS files on disk)]
    py --> state[(~/.rforge/<br>context.json)]
    py --> out[text or JSON<br>to stdout]

docs.title: Modules
docs.text: Module Public functions CLI entry point lib/discovery.py detect_ecosystem, find_r_packages, parse_description, read_description python3 -m lib.discovery --path . --format text\|json lib/deps.py build_graph, analyze_impact, get_all_dependents, get_update_order, identify_blockers python3 -m lib.deps [--path .] [--format text\|json] [graph\|impact ...]

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Walks a directory tree (depth ≤ 2 by default) for R DESCRIPTION files, parses each into a structured record, and classifies the layout as single | ecosystem | hybrid.

docs.title: CLI
docs.text:
# Text (human-readable, with emojis + tree layout)
python3 -m lib.discovery --path . --format text

# JSON (machine-readable)
python3 -m lib.discovery --path ~/projects/r-packages/active --format json

docs.title: Python API
docs.text:
from lib.discovery import detect_ecosystem

eco = detect_ecosystem(".")
print(eco.kind)           # "single" | "ecosystem" | "hybrid"
print(eco.mode)           # "minimal" | "standard" | "full" (MCP-compatible)
print([p.name for p in eco.packages])

docs.title: Returned Ecosystem dataclass
docs.text: Field Type Notes root str Absolute path that was scanned packages list[Package] One entry per DESCRIPTION found kind "single" \| "ecosystem" \| "hybrid" User-facing classification mode "minimal" \| "standard" \| "full" Preserved from rforge-mcp for wire compatibility config_found bool True if .rforge.yaml exists at root config_path str \| None Absolute path to .rforge.yaml when present

docs.title: Classification heuristic (strict)
docs.text:
• 0–1 packages → single
  • 2+ packages, no .rforge.yaml declaring otherwise → ecosystem
  • 2+ packages with kind: hybrid declared in .rforge.yaml → hybrid

hybrid is opt-in only. Filesystem heuristics (e.g., "sibling non-package dirs exist") are intentionally NOT used — too fuzzy in practice (a stray docs/ directory would flip the classification).

docs.title: Package dataclass
docs.text: Field Type Notes name str From Package: field version str From Version: field path str Package root (parent of DESCRIPTION) category "active" \| "stable" \| "archived" Inferred from path (stable/, cran/ → stable; archived/, deprecated/ → archived) description Description \| None Parsed contents of DESCRIPTION

docs.title: DESCRIPTION parser notes
docs.text:
• Handles RFC-822-style continuation lines (indented under previous field)
  • Strips version constraints: dplyr (>= 1.0) → "dplyr"
  • Filters out R itself from dep lists (it's the runtime, not a package)
  • Returns None (not an exception) on parse failure / missing Package:

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Given an Ecosystem, builds the internal dependency graph (DAG over internal packages only — external CRAN deps are filtered out), computes topological build order, detects cycles, and analyzes the downstream blast radius of changes.

docs.title: CLI subcommands
docs.text:
# Default: print the graph
python3 -m lib.deps --path . --format text
python3 -m lib.deps --path . --format text graph   # explicit

# Impact analysis (requires --package)
python3 -m lib.deps --path . --format text impact \
    --package medfit --change-type breaking

# JSON output
python3 -m lib.deps --path . --format json impact --package medfit --change-type breaking

--change-type accepts breaking | feature | fix | internal (default feature). Optional --affected-exports name1 name2 adds them to the recommendation list.

docs.title: Python API
docs.text:
from lib.discovery import detect_ecosystem
from lib.deps import build_graph, analyze_impact

eco = detect_ecosystem(".")
graph = build_graph(eco)

print(graph.layers)       # [["core"], ["impl"], ["top"]]
print(graph.circular)     # [] if no cycles, else list of cycles

impact = analyze_impact(graph, "core", change_type="breaking")
print(impact.risk_level)             # "high"
print(impact.update_sequence)        # ["core", "impl", "top"]
print(impact.estimated_work)         # "2.0h"

docs.title: Graph contract
docs.text:
• Edge convention: Edge(from_=importer, to=imported).
  • Hard edges only count for topo + cycle detection: imports and depends. suggests and linkingTo are recorded as edges but don't block layering.
  • Layers contain leaves first — Layer 0 = packages with no internal deps, Layer N = depends only on Layers 0..N−1. This is the correct build order.
  • External deps are filtered out of the graph entirely. They're still recoverable from the Ecosystem (each Package.description has the full dep lists).

docs.title: Impact heuristic
docs.text:
Input Output breaking change OR direct dependents > 2 risk_level = high Has direct dependents OR feature change risk_level = medium Otherwise (internal/fix with no dependents) risk_level = low

Work estimate (estimated_work): base + 30 × |direct| + 15 × |indirect| minutes, where base = 60 for breaking, 30 otherwise. Formatted as "X min", "X.Yh", or "X day". The estimates come straight from the original rforge-mcp heuristic (preserved for side-by-side comparability).

docs.title: End-to-end example: mediationverse
docs.text:
Real-world layout: 5 R packages under ~/projects/r-packages/active.

$ python3 -m lib.discovery --path ~/projects/r-packages/active --format text
🏗️  Ecosystem: /Users/.../r-packages/active
   Packages: 5 | mode: full | config: not found

   ├─ medfit 0.1.0
   ├─ mediationverse 0.0.0.9000
   ├─ medrobust 0.1.0.9000
   ├─ medsim 0.0.0.9000
   └─ probmed 0.0.0.9000

$ python3 -m lib.deps --path ~/projects/r-packages/active --format text
🔗 DEPENDENCY ANALYSIS

Packages: 5
Internal dependencies: 2

📊 BUILD ORDER (Topological Layers)
  Layer 1: medfit, medrobust, medsim
  Layer 2: mediationverse, probmed

→ DEPENDENCIES
  mediationverse → medfit
  probmed → medfit

🚧 BLOCKING PACKAGES
  • medfit blocks: mediationverse, probmed

📦 EXTERNAL DEPENDENCIES
  S7, checkmate, cli, dplyr, generics, ggplot2, methods, parallel,
  pbapply, rlang, stats, utils

$ python3 -m lib.deps --path ~/projects/r-packages/active \
    impact --package medfit --change-type breaking
📊 IMPACT: medfit (breaking)
  Direct dependents:   2
  Indirect dependents: 0
  Risk level:          high
  Estimated work:      2.0h
  ...

docs.title: Replacement of rforge-mcp
docs.text:
As of v1.3.0 the lib/ modules replace rforge-mcp entirely. The MCP server is no longer required (and is being archived). Anyone migrating from v1.2.x can remove the mcpServers.rforge entry from ~/.claude/settings.json — see migration/rforge-mcp-deprecation.md for the full migration table mapping old MCP tools to new lib/ modules.

Field-level wire compatibility: lib/status.py reads/writes the same .STATUS shape MCP did, and lib/init.py writes the same ~/.rforge/context.json schema MCP used — so user state migrates transparently with no copy step.

docs.title: Testing
docs.text:
python3 -m pytest tests/test_lib_discovery.py tests/test_lib_deps.py tests/test_lib_status.py tests/test_lib_init.py -v

65 cases cover DESCRIPTION edge cases, FS traversal, classification, graph construction, cycle detection, impact heuristics, blockers, .STATUS parsing + health-score math, and ~/.rforge/context.json round-trip with idempotency. Integrated into tests/test-all.sh (full plugin suite — 23 checks).

docs.title: RForge Quick Start Guide
docs.text: Get started with RForge in 5 minutes. This guide walks you through installation, basic usage, and your first analysis.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before installing RForge, ensure you have:

  1. Claude Code - RForge is a Claude Code plugin
# Check Claude Code version
claude --version
  1. R Environment (>= 4.0.0)
# Check R version
R --version
  1. Required R Packages
# Install in R
install.packages(c("devtools", "testthat"))

# Optional (for coverage)
install.packages("covr")

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: v1.3.0 note: RForge is now fully self-contained. The MCP server is no longer required (and is being archived). If you previously had an mcpServers.rforge entry in ~/.claude/settings.json, see migration/rforge-mcp-deprecation.md.

docs.title: Step 1: Install RForge Plugin
docs.text:
# Via Claude Code marketplace (recommended)
/plugin marketplace add Data-Wise/rforge
/plugin install rforge

# Or via Homebrew (macOS)
brew install data-wise/tap/rforge

# Or via npm
npm install -g @data-wise/rforge-plugin

# Or from source (development)
git clone https://github.com/Data-Wise/rforge.git
cd rforge
./scripts/install.sh --dev

docs.title: Step 2: Verify Python 3.10+
docs.text:
python3 --version    # Expect 3.10 or newer

The lib/ modules run via python3 -m lib.<tool>; modern macOS / Linux distros ship with a compatible Python by default.

docs.title: Step 3: Restart Claude Code
docs.text:
# Exit and restart Claude Code
# The plugin will be automatically loaded

docs.title: Step 4: Verify Installation
docs.text:
# Navigate to any R package directory
cd ~/path/to/your-r-package

# Run a quick status check
/rforge:status

You should see output showing your package health status. If you get an error, check that: - The plugin loaded (ls ~/.claude/plugins/rforge) - You're in an R package directory (has DESCRIPTION file) - Python 3.10+ is on PATH (python3 --version)

docs.title: Quick Status Check (<10 seconds)
docs.text:
/rforge:status

Example output: 

╭──────────────────────────────────────────╮
│ 📊 RForge Status - mypackage             │
├──────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                          │
│ Health Score: 85/100 ✅                  │
│                                          │
│ 📦 Package: mypackage v0.1.0             │
│ 📂 Path: /path/to/mypackage              │
│ 🌿 Git: main (clean)                     │
│                                          │
│ ✅ Dependencies: 5 packages, all current │
│ ✅ Tests: 42 passing                     │
│ ⚠️  Coverage: 78% (target: 80%)          │
│                                          │
╰──────────────────────────────────────────╯

docs.title: Analyze Package (<30 seconds)
docs.text:
/rforge:analyze "Check package health before CRAN submission"

This performs: - Dependency analysis - Test execution status - Documentation completeness - NAMESPACE validation - Git status check

docs.title: Mode System
docs.text:
RForge supports 4 analysis modes with different time budgets:

Mode Command Time Use Case default /rforge:status <10s Quick daily checks debug /rforge:analyze --mode debug <120s Detailed diagnostics optimize /rforge:analyze --mode optimize <180s Performance analysis release /rforge:analyze --mode release <300s CRAN preparation

Example: 

# Debug mode for investigating test failures
/rforge:analyze --mode debug "Why are tests failing on CI?"

# Release mode for comprehensive CRAN check
/rforge:analyze --mode release "Prepare for CRAN submission"

docs.title: 1. Daily Development Check
docs.text:
# Morning check-in
/rforge:status

# If issues found
/rforge:analyze --mode debug "Investigate warnings"

docs.title: 2. Before Committing Code
docs.text:
# Quick pre-commit validation
/rforge:status

# Check impact of your changes
/rforge:cascade "Updated base dependency version"

docs.title: 3. CRAN Preparation
docs.text:
# Comprehensive release check
/rforge:analyze --mode release "CRAN submission checklist"

# Plan release sequence (for ecosystems)
/rforge:release

# Generate dependency graph
/rforge:deps

docs.title: 4. Ecosystem Management
docs.text:
# Detect project structure
/rforge:detect

# Analyze cross-package impact
/rforge:impact "Changed API in base package"

# Plan coordinated updates
/rforge:cascade "Major version bump in dependency"

docs.title: Output Formats
docs.text: RForge supports 3 output formats:

docs.title: Terminal (Default)
docs.text:
Rich colors, emojis, and formatted tables for human readability.

/rforge:status

docs.title: JSON
docs.text:
Machine-readable with metadata envelope for automation.

/rforge:status --format json > status.json

docs.title: Markdown
docs.text:
Documentation-ready for reports and READMEs.

/rforge:status --format markdown > STATUS.md

docs.title: Project Structure Detection
docs.text: RForge automatically detects three project types:

docs.title: Single Package
docs.text:
mypackage/
├── DESCRIPTION
├── NAMESPACE
├── R/
├── man/
└── tests/

docs.title: Ecosystem (Multiple Packages)
docs.text:
my-ecosystem/
├── base-package/
│   └── DESCRIPTION
├── extension-1/
│   └── DESCRIPTION
└── extension-2/
    └── DESCRIPTION

docs.title: Hybrid (Mixed Content)
docs.text:
my-project/
├── packages/
│   ├── pkg-1/DESCRIPTION
│   └── pkg-2/DESCRIPTION
├── analysis/
└── docs/

Use /rforge:detect to see how RForge categorizes your project.

docs.title: "python3: command not found"
docs.text:
Solution: 

# Verify Python 3.10+ is installed
python3 --version

# macOS: install via Homebrew
brew install python@3.12

# Linux: install via your distro's package manager

docs.title: "Not an R package directory"
docs.text:
Solution: 

# Ensure you're in a directory with DESCRIPTION file
ls -la DESCRIPTION

# Or specify package path explicitly
cd /path/to/your-package

docs.title: "R CMD not found"
docs.text:
Solution: 

# Check R is in PATH
which R

# Add R to PATH if needed (macOS/Linux)
export PATH="/usr/local/bin:$PATH"

# Restart Claude Code after updating PATH

docs.title: Performance Issues
docs.text:
If analysis is slow:

  1. Use faster modes:
/rforge:status  # Instead of /rforge:analyze
  1. Check for large .Rcheck directories:
find . -name "*.Rcheck" -type d
# Remove if found
rm -rf *.Rcheck
  1. Limit scope for ecosystems:
/rforge:status mypackage  # Specify single package

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
Now that you're set up, explore:

  • Commands Reference - All 15 RForge commands
  • Architecture Guide - How RForge works internally

docs.title: Common Commands Cheat Sheet
docs.text:
# Status and analysis
/rforge:status                    # Quick health check
/rforge:analyze                   # Balanced analysis
/rforge:quick                     # Ultra-fast (<10s)
/rforge:thorough                  # Comprehensive (2-5min)

# Ecosystem management
/rforge:detect                    # Detect project structure
/rforge:cascade                   # Plan coordinated updates
/rforge:impact                    # Analyze change impact
/rforge:release                   # Plan CRAN release sequence
/rforge:deps                      # Visualize dependencies

# Documentation
/rforge:doc-check                 # Check documentation drift
/rforge:complete                  # Mark tasks complete
/rforge:capture                   # Quick capture ideas
/rforge:next                      # Get next task suggestion

# Health checks
/rforge:ecosystem-health          # Ecosystem health metrics
/rforge:rpkg-check                # R CMD check integration

docs.title: Getting Help
docs.text:
• GitHub Issues: Data-Wise/rforge/issues
  • Documentation Site: data-wise.github.io/rforge
  • npm Package: @data-wise/rforge-plugin

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Common errors and how to resolve them. Symptoms are listed first; click through to the section that matches what you're seeing.

docs.title: Quick navigation
docs.text:
• Install / setup issues
  • Command not found / not loading
  • Path errors from lib/ modules
  • Missing R / Rscript
  • init / context state issues
  • Leftover rforge-mcp signals
  • Hook unexpectedly blocks an edit
  • Tests fail after a fresh install

If your issue isn't covered, file an issue: https://github.com/Data-Wise/rforge/issues.

docs.title: npm install -g @data-wise/rforge-plugin fails with 404 on rforge-mcp
docs.text:
You're on a plugin version older than v1.2.0. The peer-dep was removed in v1.2.0; upgrade with:

npm install -g @data-wise/rforge-plugin@latest

If you're already on the latest and still seeing this, check package.json of your local clone — the peerDependencies block should be absent.

docs.title: brew install --HEAD data-wise/tap/rforge succeeds but plugin doesn't load
docs.text:
Two diagnoses:

# 1) Did the install script create the plugin symlink?
ls -la ~/.claude/plugins/rforge
# Expected: a symlink to /opt/homebrew/opt/rforge/libexec

# 2) Is Claude Code seeing the plugin?
# In Claude Code: type "/help" and search for "/rforge:"

If the symlink is missing, the post-install script's ln step failed (usually macOS extended-attribute permissions). Fix manually:

ln -sf "$(brew --prefix)/opt/rforge/libexec" ~/.claude/plugins/rforge

Then restart Claude Code.

docs.title: /rforge:* commands don't appear in /help
docs.text:
Most likely Claude Code hasn't picked up the plugin since install. In order:

  1. Restart Claude Code (quit + reopen)
  2. If still missing: ls ~/.claude/plugins/rforge/commands/ — should list 15 .md files
  3. If empty: re-run install (see Install issues)

docs.title: /rforge:detect runs but produces no output
docs.text:
The plugin command is a markdown prompt; Claude has to invoke the underlying Python module. Check Claude has Bash access (in Claude Code permissions) and that lib/discovery.py is reachable:

python3 -m lib.discovery --path . --format text
# Run this from your project's repo root (or the plugin install dir)

If Python fails too, see Path errors.

docs.title: error: path does not exist: /some/path
docs.text: You passed a --path that doesn't exist (or has a typo). discovery, status, and init all validate the path before doing anything. Fix the path or omit --path (defaults to current directory).

docs.title: ImportError: attempted relative import with no known parent package
docs.text:
You ran a lib/ module as a script:

python3 lib/deps.py    # ❌ won't work

The modules use relative imports (from .discovery import ...) which require running them as a package:

python3 -m lib.deps    # ✅ works

This is intentional — it forces the package layout that prevents sys.path collisions. Use -m lib.<module> everywhere.

docs.title: ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'lib'
docs.text:
You're running from a directory that doesn't contain the plugin's lib/ as a subdirectory. The -m lib.<module> form requires lib/ to be on sys.path, which Python derives from the current working directory.

# ❌ from anywhere
python3 -m lib.discovery

# ✅ from the plugin install dir
cd "$(brew --prefix)/opt/rforge/libexec"
python3 -m lib.discovery --path /your/r/package

The plugin commands handle this internally by cd-ing to the right place before invoking. If you're getting this error from a slash command, it's a bug — file an issue.

docs.title: lib.status reports unknown for every package's check/test
docs.text: This is expected in v1.3.0 — the R subprocess integration is deferred to v1.4.0. status currently just reads .STATUS files; it doesn't shell out to R CMD check or run test suites. The unknown placeholder is intentional.

docs.title: Future v1.4.0 behavior
docs.text: When the 4-mode contract lands, missing R will degrade gracefully: default and debug modes will continue to work; optimize and release modes will warn and skip the R-requiring checks. Until then, nothing in v1.3.0 requires R installed.

docs.title: /rforge:init says "Already initialized" but I'm in a different package
docs.text:
The ~/.rforge/context.json file is per-user, not per-package. It tracks one active context at a time. To re-init for a new package:

/rforge:init    # in the new package directory

The state file gets overwritten with the new package's info. There's no "multi-context" mode — by design (matches MCP server semantics so migration is transparent).

If you want a clean slate:

rm ~/.rforge/context.json

Then re-init.

docs.title: Permission denied writing ~/.rforge/context.json
docs.text:
The directory permissions are off. Fix:

mkdir -p ~/.rforge
chmod 755 ~/.rforge

Then re-run /rforge:init.

docs.title: npm warns about an orphaned rforge-mcp install
docs.text: You upgraded the plugin but didn't uninstall the old MCP server. Walk through the migration tutorial — Step 3 covers the uninstall.

docs.title: ~/.claude/settings.json still has mcpServers.rforge
docs.text: Same fix — see migration tutorial Step 2. The plugin doesn't need this entry as of v1.3.0; removing it eliminates a stale config reference.

docs.title: Claude Code logs say "MCP server rforge failed to start"
docs.text:
The MCP server config is still in ~/.claude/settings.json but the binary isn't installed (because you uninstalled rforge-mcp). Either:

  • Remove the config block (recommended — see migration Step 2), or
  • Reinstall rforge-mcp if you specifically want it back

docs.title: "Refusing to edit roxygen-generated man/*.Rd file"
docs.text:
This is the v1.2.0 pretooluse hook doing its job. man/*.Rd files are auto-generated by devtools::document() — hand-edits get lost the next time you run document(). Edit the @param/@return/@examples roxygen comments in the corresponding R/ file instead, then run devtools::document() to regenerate.

If you need to bypass this in an emergency (e.g., the regenerator itself is broken):

# Disable the hook for one session
mv ~/.claude/hooks/pretooluse.py.bak ~/.claude/hooks/pretooluse.py.bak.disabled

Re-enable when done. See Hooks & Skills for details on the 4 hook rules.

docs.title: Branch-guard blocks my commit on dev
docs.text:
You're trying to commit code (not docs/config) directly to dev. Use a feature worktree:

git worktree add ~/.git-worktrees/rforge/feature-<name> -b feature/<name> dev
cd ~/.git-worktrees/rforge/feature-<name>
# do your work here, then PR back to dev

Or, for a one-shot emergency:

touch .claude/allow-once    # bypass expires after 5 minutes

See the global CLAUDE.md "Branch Guard & Git Hooks" section for the full protection rules.

docs.title: Tests fail after a fresh install
bash tests/test-all.sh reports Lib: pytest suite ❌
docs.text:
The lib/ tests need pytest. Install it:

pip install pytest

Then re-run. If you don't have pip, install Python's standard build (>= 3.10) — the lib/ modules use type hints that require it.

docs.title: Lib: reference docs in sync with source ❌
docs.text:
You edited a module's docstring but didn't regenerate the reference docs. Fix:

python3 scripts/gen_lib_reference.py

Then commit the regenerated docs/reference/*.md files. The CI check (gen_lib_reference.py --check) compares the generated output against disk; any mismatch is treated as drift.

docs.title: A specific pytest case fails after my edit
docs.text:
Run it in isolation for the error message:

python3 -m pytest tests/test_lib_<module>.py::<test_name> -v

If the failure is a real regression in your edit, the test name tells you which contract you broke. If it's a test-only issue, file as a bug.

docs.title: Still stuck?
docs.text:
• Search existing issues: https://github.com/Data-Wise/rforge/issues?q=
  • File a new issue: include rforge version (brew list --versions rforge), Claude Code version, the exact command you ran, and the full error output. Reproducer is gold.
  • Check the CHANGELOG: maybe the behavior you're seeing was a documented breaking change in a recent version.

docs.title: rforge-mcp is deprecated
docs.text: This MCP server has been absorbed into the rforge Claude Code plugin as of plugin version 1.3.0 (released 2026-05-11). The functionality is now provided in pure Python under the plugin's lib/ directory — no MCP server is required at runtime.

docs.title: TL;DR
docs.text:
• Update the rforge plugin to v1.3.0 or later.
  • Remove the mcpServers.rforge entry from ~/.claude/settings.json.
  • Uninstall any local rforge-mcp package install.
  • This repository is now archived (read-only).

docs.title: What changed
docs.text:
The MCP tool surface previously exposed by rforge-mcp is now available directly inside the plugin. Each former tool maps cleanly onto a Python module under lib/ and (where applicable) a slash command.

Old (rforge-mcp tool) New (rforge plugin) rforge_discovery python3 -m lib.discovery rforge_deps python3 -m lib.deps rforge_status python3 -m lib.status rforge_init python3 -m lib.init rforge_quick_* tools /rforge:quick (composed lib calls)

The slash commands (/rforge:status, /rforge:deps, /rforge:quick, ...) continue to work exactly as before — they now invoke the absorbed Python modules instead of crossing an MCP boundary.

docs.title: Migration steps for existing users
docs.text:
1. Update the rforge plugin to v1.3.0 or later via your plugin manager or git pull in your plugin checkout.

  2. Remove the MCP server entry from ~/.claude/settings.json:

// DELETE this block if present:
"mcpServers": {
  "rforge": { ... }
}

Leave any other mcpServers entries intact. If mcpServers becomes empty after the deletion, you can remove the empty object entirely.

  1. Uninstall the rforge-mcp package if you installed it locally:
npm uninstall -g rforge-mcp

(rforge-mcp was never published to a package registry, so this only applies if you installed from a local clone via npm link or similar.)

  1. State file: the absorbed lib/init.py writes to ~/.rforge/context.json using the same schema as rforge-mcp. Existing state migrates transparently — no manual conversion needed.

docs.title: Why the absorption
docs.text:
The MCP server added a process boundary and a Node.js runtime dependency for a tool that was, by line count, mostly file parsers and dependency-graph walkers. Moving that logic into pure Python inside the plugin gives users:

  • One fewer thing to install (no rforge-mcp package).
  • Zero network or IPC dependencies at runtime.
  • Direct CLI access via python3 -m lib.<tool> for scripting and debugging.
  • A single source of truth for the discovery / deps / status logic, instead of split implementations between plugin and MCP server.

docs.title: This repository is archived
docs.text:
This repo is read-only as of [DATE TO BE FILLED IN AT ARCHIVE TIME]. Issues and PRs are not accepted. For bugs in the absorbed implementations, please file at https://github.com/Data-Wise/rforge.

The source history remains visible and clonable for anyone who needs to reference the original MCP implementation.

docs.title: Final version
docs.text: rforge-mcp's final version (whatever was in main at archive time) remains available via this repo's source history. No further releases are planned.

docs.title: v1.3.0 post-merge checklist (cross-repo work)
docs.text:
After the rforge plugin v1.3.0 PR merges to dev/main, complete these out-of-tree steps. None of them touch the rforge repo itself — they all operate on the (now-obsolete) data-wise/rforge-mcp repo.

Why post-merge? Archiving rforge-mcp before v1.3.0 lands would leave the replacement under review while the original is read-only — bad UX for anyone watching the org. Wait for the merge, then run these steps.

docs.title: 1. Push DEPRECATED.md to rforge-mcp
docs.text:
# Adjust this path to wherever you have rforge-mcp cloned locally.
# Common locations: ~/projects/data-wise/rforge-mcp or ~/code/rforge-mcp
cd ~/projects/data-wise/rforge-mcp

git checkout main
git pull

# Copy the drafted text from the merged rforge PR into ./DEPRECATED.md
# (adjust the source path if your rforge clone lives elsewhere)
cp ~/projects/dev-tools/rforge/docs/migration/rforge-mcp-deprecation.md ./DEPRECATED.md

# Edit DEPRECATED.md: replace [DATE TO BE FILLED IN AT ARCHIVE TIME]
# with today's date (YYYY-MM-DD).
$EDITOR DEPRECATED.md

git add DEPRECATED.md
git commit -m "docs: deprecation notice — absorbed into rforge plugin v1.3.0"
git push origin main

docs.title: 2. Archive the GitHub repo
docs.text:
gh repo archive data-wise/rforge-mcp --yes

This makes the repo read-only. Issues and PRs are blocked. The repo remains visible and clonable. Reversible via the GitHub UI if needed (Settings → Danger Zone → Unarchive).

docs.title: 3. (Optional) Note to existing local users
docs.text: If you have shell aliases, npm-linked binaries, or shortcuts pointing at a local rforge-mcp install, remove them. The plugin now provides everything via python3 -m lib.<tool> and the /rforge:* slash commands.

docs.title: 4. Update your own settings.json
docs.text:
If your personal ~/.claude/settings.json has an mcpServers.rforge entry, remove it manually. We don't auto-edit user settings. Example:

// Before:
"mcpServers": {
  "rforge": { "command": "...", "args": [...] },
  "other-server": { ... }
}

// After:
"mcpServers": {
  "other-server": { ... }
}

docs.title: Verification
docs.text:
After completing steps 1 and 2:

  • gh repo view data-wise/rforge-mcp should show the archived banner.
  • Visiting https://github.com/data-wise/rforge-mcp should display the "This repository has been archived..." notice at the top.
  • gh api repos/data-wise/rforge-mcp --jq .archived should return true.
  • The DEPRECATED.md file should be visible at the root of the rforge-mcp repo on the main branch.

docs.title: What's NOT needed
docs.text: No npm, Homebrew, or PyPI cleanup is required — rforge-mcp was never published to any package registry. The only artifacts are the GitHub repo itself and any local clones / npm link installs on individual machines.

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
If something goes wrong:

  • DEPRECATED.md commit: git revert the commit on rforge-mcp main.
  • Archive: GitHub UI → repo Settings → Danger Zone → Unarchive repository. The repo becomes writable again immediately.

docs.title: Module overview
docs.text:
R ecosystem dependency analysis.

Builds a dependency graph across the packages discovered by discovery.py, exposes topological layers (build order), detects circular dependencies, and analyzes downstream impact of a change to one package.

Pure Python — no R subprocess. Ported from rforge-mcp/dist/tools/deps/{deps,impact}.js (Path B Phase B.1).

Edge convention: Edge(from_=importer, to=imported). Topological layers contain leaves first (deepest deps), which is the correct order to build in.

Usage (CLI, from repo root): python3 -m lib.deps --path /path/to/eco --format text python3 -m lib.deps impact --package medfit --change-type breaking \ --path /path/to/eco --format json

Usage (Python API): from lib.discovery import detect_ecosystem from lib.deps import build_graph, analyze_impact eco = detect_ecosystem(".") graph = build_graph(eco) impact = analyze_impact(graph, "medfit", change_type="breaking")

docs.title: class DepGraph
docs.text:
Dependency graph for an ecosystem.

Attributes: nodes: package names in the ecosystem. edges: directed edges (importer → imported). layers: topological layers, leaves first. Layer i+1 depends only on layers ≤ i. Within a layer, packages can be built in parallel. circular: any cycles detected (each cycle is a list of node names forming the loop, not closed — i.e. last → first is implied).

Field Type Default nodes list[str] — edges list[Edge] — layers list[list[str]] — circular list[list[str]] list()

docs.text:
R ecosystem discovery.

Walks a directory tree looking for R DESCRIPTION files, parses them into structured Package records, and classifies the result as a single package, an ecosystem, or a hybrid layout.

Pure Python — no R subprocess, no external deps beyond the stdlib. Ported from rforge-mcp/dist/tools/discovery/detect.js (Path B Phase B.1).

docs.title: Module overview
docs.text:
RForge context initialization.

Ports rforge_init from the MCP server (src/tools/init/index.ts and src/utils/context-manager.ts) to a pure-Python module. Persists a per-user JSON state file at ~/.rforge/context.json that records the currently-active R package, when it was first detected, and lightweight workflow metadata that other tools (plan, release, …) read on entry.

The MCP behavior is faithfully replicated, with one deliberate addition: production callers leave home=None and we resolve $HOME; tests pass an explicit home= to redirect the state file into tmp_path, so the real ~/.rforge/ is never touched.

Field names match MCP exactly (snake_case): package, version, path, detected_at, current_workflow, last_tool, last_plan, initialized. The package key is intentionally the raw name (not package_name) so a context.json produced by the Node MCP server migrates as-is.

Usage (CLI): python3 -m lib.init --path . --format text python3 -m lib.init --path /pkg --quick --format json python3 -m lib.init --path . --home /tmp/sandbox # never touches real ~

Usage (API): from lib.init import init_context result = init_context(".") print(result.state_path, result.was_initialized)

docs.title: Module overview
docs.text:
R ecosystem status aggregation.

Walks an ecosystem (via discovery.py) and produces a dashboard-style snapshot: per-package status, ecosystem health score (0-100), and any blocking issues. Reads each package's .STATUS file (if present) for human-curated progress notes.

Pure Python — no R subprocess. Ported from rforge-mcp/dist/tools/discovery/status.js (Path B Phase B.2). Check and test statuses are stubbed as "unknown" for parity with the MCP server; a future increment will wire them up to actual R CMD check output.

Usage (CLI, from repo root): python3 -m lib.status --path . --format text python3 -m lib.status --path /path/to/eco --format json

Usage (Python API): from lib.status import aggregate_status snapshot = aggregate_status(".") print(snapshot.health_score)

def aggregate_status(ecosystem_path: 'str | os.PathLike' = '.') -> 'EcosystemStatus'

Build an EcosystemStatus snapshot for the given path.

Discovers packages via detect_ecosystem, reads each package's .STATUS (if present), and computes a health score.

docs.title: calculate_health_score()
docs.text:
def calculate_health_score(packages: 'list[PackageStatus]', now: 'Optional[datetime]' = None) -> 'int'

Compute the 0-100 ecosystem health score.

Faithful port of the MCP server's algorithm: each package gets a slice of 100/N points, and deductions apply for unknown/failing check or test statuses plus a staleness penalty for .STATUS files older than 14 days. Empty ecosystems are perfect (score=100) by convention — the MCP server returned 0 here, which conflated "no data" with "broken"; the orchestrator chose the saner default for B.2.
